coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric detection of copper ions and alkaline phosphatase activity based on semiconducting polymer dots assembled with rhodamine B hydrazide
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Junyong
Mei, Han
Gao, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The rational surface functionalization of semiconducting polymer dots (Pdots) has attracted much attention to extend their applications in fabricating chemo/biosensing platform. In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform using functionalized Pdots as probes for fluorescence signal transmission has been designed for sensing Cu(Ⅱ) and activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) with high selectivity and enhanced sensitivity. The highly fluorescent Pdots were firstly prepared with Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-co-(1,4-benzo-{2,1′,3}-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) via nanoprecipitation method, and then assembled with non-fluorescent rhodamine B hydrazide (RB-hy), which shows special binding activity to Cu(Ⅱ), through adsorption process to obtain functionalized nanohybrids, Pdots@RB-hy. As thus, a FRET donors/acceptors pair, in which PFBT Pdots act as energy donors while RB-hy-Cu(II) complexes act as energy acceptors were constructed. On the basis of the varies in fluorescence intensities of donors/acceptors in the presence of different amounts of Cu(II), a ratiometric method for sensing Cu(II) has been proposed. The proposed ratiometric Cu(II) sensor shows a good linear detection range from 0.05 to 5μM with a detection limit of 15nM. Furthermore, using the Pdots@RB-hy-Cu(II) system as signal transducer, a ratiometric sensing for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has also been established with pyrophosphate (PPi) as substrates. The constructed ratiometric sensor of ALP activity displays a linear detection range from 0.005 to 15UL−1 with a detection limit of 0.0018UL−1. The sensor was further successfully used for ALP activity detection in human serum with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Semiconducting polymer dots
Rhodamine B hydrazide
Copper (II)
Alkaline phosphatase
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PFBT Pdots and Pdots@RB-hy
Procedures for fluorescence detection of Cu (II)
Procedures for fluorescence detection of ALP activity
Ratiometric sensor of Cu(II) with PFBT Pdots@RB-hy
Ratiometric assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity with PFBT Pdots@RB-hy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21575004, 21605001), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-12-0599), the project sponsored by SRF for ROCS, SEM, and the Foundation for Innovation Team of Bioanalytical Chemistry of Anhui Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy(A) and dynamic light scattering measurements(B) of PFBT Pdots. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Pdots; (b) Pdots +3.5μM Cu(II); (c) Pdots@RB-hy; (d) Pdots@RB-hy +0.5μM Cu(II); (e) Pdots@RB-hy +3.5μM Cu(II) in HEPES buffer. The concentrations for PFBT Pdots and Pdots@RB-hy are 1.0μgmL−1. (D) Emission spectrum of RB-hy(a), absorbance spectrum of RB-hy(b), absorbance spectrum of RB-hy-Cu(II)(c), emission spectrum of Pdots (d) and the emission spectrum of RB-hy-Cu(II)(e).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system with various concentrations of Cu(II) and the solution color under UV illumination (inset) in HEPES buffer. (B) The intensity ratio (I583/I544) of the sensing system versus the Cu(II) concentrations and the calibration regression curve (inset) in HEPES buffer. (C) Absorption spectra of the sensing system with various Cu(II) concentrations and the solution color under natural light illumination (inset) in HEPES buffer. (D) The intensity ratio (I583/I544) towards various interference species with the concentration of 35μM in the presence of 3.5μM Cu(II) in HEPES buffer. The concentration of Pdots@RB-hy used for above experiments is 1.0μgmL−1.(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A)Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system at various ALP concentrations and the solution color under UV illumination (inset) in HEPES buffer. (B)The intensity ratio (I583/I544) of sensing system versus the ALP concentrations and the standard curve (inset). (C) The intensity ratio (I583/I544) towards various substances with a concentration of 100UL-1 in the presence of 10UL-1ALP in HEPES buffer. Pdots@RB-hy: 1.0μgmL-1; Cu(II): 5μM; PPi: 10μM.(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The results of determination of the sample by the standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric detection of copper ions and alkaline phosphatase activity based on semiconducting polymer dots assembled with rhodamine B hydrazide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Functionalized Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, Laboratory of Optical Probes and Bioelectrocatalysis (LOPAB), College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Functionalized Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, Laboratory of Optical Probes and Bioelectrocatalysis (LOPAB), College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational surface functionalization of semiconducting polymer dots (Pdots) has attracted much attention to extend their applications in fabricating chemo/biosensing platform. In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform using functionalized Pdots as probes for fluorescence signal transmission has been designed for sensing Cu(Ⅱ) and activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) with high selectivity and enhanced sensitivity. The highly fluorescent Pdots were firstly prepared with Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-co-(1,4-benzo-{2,1′,3}-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) via nanoprecipitation method, and then assembled with non-fluorescent rhodamine B hydrazide (RB-hy), which shows special binding activity to Cu(Ⅱ), through adsorption process to obtain functionalized nanohybrids, Pdots@RB-hy. As thus, a FRET donors/acceptors pair, in which PFBT Pdots act as energy donors while RB-hy-Cu(II) complexes act as energy acceptors were constructed. On the basis of the varies in fluorescence intensities of donors/acceptors in the presence of different amounts of Cu(II), a ratiometric method for sensing Cu(II) has been proposed. The proposed ratiometric Cu(II) sensor shows a good linear detection range from 0.05 to 5μM with a detection limit of 15nM. Furthermore, using the Pdots@RB-hy-Cu(II) system as signal transducer, a ratiometric sensing for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has also been established with pyrophosphate (PPi) as substrates. The constructed ratiometric sensor of ALP activity displays a linear detection range from 0.005 to 15UL−1 with a detection limit of 0.0018UL−1. The sensor was further successfully used for ALP activity detection in human serum with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis, which relays on special fluorescent probes for signal readout, is one of the most popular techniques for chemo/biosensing, labeling, and imaging in chemical, biological, medical and diagnostic fields (Chan and Nie, 1998). The characteristics of the used fluorophores including fluorescence brightness and photostability, may have important effects on the analytical performances, such as the detection limit, sensitivity, spatiotemporal resolution and analytical reliability (Wu and Chiu, 2013). In this respect, the development of novel fluorescent materials is of utmost importance. Comparing to commonly used fluorophores including organic dyes, quantum dots, and luminescent nanoclusters, semiconducting polymer dots (Pdots) that mainly consist of π-conjugated polymers have been emerged as excellent candidates and are more competent to the fluorescent probes of constructing chemo/biosensing due to their fascinating performances, such as extraordinary fluorescence brightness, ampliﬁed energy transfer, flexibility, excellent photostability, high emission rate and non-toxicity (Chen et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Massey et al., 2015; Peng and Chiu, 2015; Tian et al., 2010; Wu and Chiu, 2013). Currently, studies on rational functionalization of Pdots to expand their applications in chemo/biosensing are highly desired and have attracted much attention (Chan et al., 2011b; Huang et al., 2014; Li and Liu, 2012; Pecher and Mecking, 2010; Sun et al., 2016a, 2016b; Tuncel and Demir, 2010; Wang et al., 2016; Wu and Chiu, 2013; Wu et al., 2007; Ye et al., 2011).

Copper ion (Cu(II)) plays important roles in the human body and its quantitative detection is great importance in biological samples because its excess will lead to different pathological consequences (Strausak et al., 2001). Among the variously developed Cu(II) sensors, detection schemes based on fluorescence have attracted increasing attention due to the inherent merits of fluorescent methods, such as good sensitivity, high reliability, and in situ cellular imaging (Kumar et al., 2011; Shirasaki et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). Nevertheless, some of these sensors showed low selectivity to some transition metal ions such as Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Pb2+ and Hg2+(Chan et al., 2011a; Hu et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2012; Xiong et al., 2013), which limited their further applications in practical sample analysis. On the other hand, alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1), a nonspecific hydrolase present in body tissues (Miller et al., 2010), has been widely used as an important indicator for clinical diagnostics (Kong et al., 2013). In recent years, several fluorescent sensors for sensitive determination of ALP have been reported (Deng et al., 2015; Kong et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). However, most of these biosensors are non-ratiometric modes and rely on measuring the changes of ﬂuorescence intensity at a single wavelength (Jia et al., 2010; Lin and Tseng, 2013; Ma et al., 2016; Qian et al., 2015a, 2015b; Ye et al., 2011), which inevitably encounters intensity fluctuations induced by instrumental or environmental factors (Li et al., 2013). Ratiometric fluorescent sensors, which measure the relative changes of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths, provide a candidate method to reduce and overcome these fluctuations (Dai et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2014).

Furtherly, when pyrophosphate (PPi), one of the natural substrates of ALP, was introduced into the Pdots@RB-hy/Cu(II) analytical system, the FRET between PFBT Pdots and RB-hy/Cu(II) was suppressed due to the competitive interaction between Cu(II) and PPi. However, PPi can be hydrolized into phosphate (Pi), which can not coordinate with Cu(II) to form complex, with the catalytic effect of ALP. Combining these principles and the catalytic hydrolysis of pyrophosphate PPi by ALP, a ratiometric sensing for ALP activity has been also established ( Scheme 2). These ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on PFBT Pdots for sensing Cu(II) and ALP activity display attractive selectivity, enhanced sensitivity and excellent reliability, and were further used in practical samples analysis with satisfactory results.

Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a quartz cell, respectively. Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) were obtained from a Hitachi HT-7700 transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were measured by a ZS90 zetasizer (Malvern, UK).

Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-co-(1,4-benzo-{2,1′,3}-thiadiazole)](PFBT, MW 164 000, polydispersity 3.4) was purchased from ADS Dyes, Inc. (Quebec, Canada). Rhodamine B hydrazide (RB-hy), 2-(4-(2-hydroxyethyl)−1-piperazinyl) ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) and tetrahydrofuran (THF, anhydrous, ≥99.9%, inhibitor-free) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. All other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the commonly employed reagents in this study are of at least analytical purity and used without further puriﬁcation. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PFBT Pdots and Pdots@RB-hy
ce:section.ce:para: The PFBT Pdots were synthesized according to the literature (Clafton et al., 2010; Harbron et al., 2009; Wu et al., 2006). Briefly, 10mg PFBT were dissolved in 10mL of inhibitor-free THF by stirring overnight under inert atmosphere and then diluted to obtain PFBT solution with a concentration of 20μgmL−1. A 2mL of PFBT solution was quickly added to 8mL of ultrapure water under sonicating condition. After that, the THF was removed by rotary evaporation at 75°C, and the solution was filtered through a 0.2µm cellulose acetate membrane filter to remove any aggregates formed during preparation. The resulting PFBT Pdots solution was concentrated through the centrifugal filtration device (Amicon® Ultra-4, MWCO: 100kDa) to obtain 50μgmL−1 PFBT Pdots for following usage. In a typical procedure for preparing Pdots@RB-hy nanohybrids, 2mL of PFBT Pdots (50μgmL−1) containing 0.1% polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW 3350) and 1mL of RB-hy (1mM) were mixed and then incubated at 37 ºC for 2h. The Pdots@RB-hy nanohybrids were seperated from the mixture by centrifugal filtration device. The resultant nanohybrids were redispersed in 3mL of ultrapure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescence detection of Cu (II)
ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of colorimetric tubes, equivalent volumes of Pdots@RB-hy nanohybrids (30µL) were firstly added and then various concentrations of Cu (II) were added, respectively. Afterwards, the mixtures were diluted to 1mL with HEPES buffer (10mM, pH 7.0; acetonitrile-water: 1/5, v/v) and incubated at 37ºC for 30min. The fluorescence spectra were collected under the excitation of 450nm at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescence detection of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, different amounts of ALP were firstly incubated with pyrophosphate (PPi) (10µM) at 37°C for the hydrolysis of PPi. After 30min incubation, Cu (II) (5µM) was added into the reaction solution and incubated for another 10min. Afterwards, 30µL of Pdots@RB-hy nanohybrids were then added into the mixture solution containing ALP, PPi and Cu(II). The mixture solution was diluted to 1mL and mixed thoroughly for 30min, and then the fluorescence spectra were collected under excitation of 450nm.

For the detection of ALP in human serum, freshly human blood samples contributed by the hospital of Anhui normal university were seperated using commonly used standard procedures to get the serum. Under the identical conditions described above, 10µL of human serum samples were used for assaying free ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensor of Cu(II) with PFBT Pdots@RB-hy
ce:section.ce:para: To realize our design for ratiometric sensing of Cu(II), we firstly synthesized the PFBT Pdots via nanoprecipitation according to a reported procedure (Wu et al., 2013) with small modifications. The prepared PFBT Pdots were characterized by TEM and DLS. As show in Fig. 1A and B, the as-prepared PFBT Pdots are approximately spherical in shape and possess fairly uniform size of 22.4nm. RB-hy was assembled on PFBT Pdots via simple adsorption to form PFBT Pdots@RB-hy hybrid nanocomposites. The bare Pdots show excellent colloidal stability in deionized water due to the partially ionized defects resulted from the oxidation of semiconducting polymers during Pdots formation (Clafton et al., 2010), which is favorable to electrostatic interaction between Pdots and RB-hy. In addition, PFBT Pdots are intrinsically hydrophobic nanoparticles and therefore possess large hydrophobic surfaces that are prone to nonspecific binding to RB-hy (water-insoluble). By combining the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, RB-hy molecules were easily assembled on PFBT Pdots to form stable Pdots@RB-hy hybrid nanocomposites. The assembly process can be confirmed by the change of surface zeta potential of Pdots. The zeta potentials of bare PFBT Pdots and PFBT Pdots@RB-hy were determined to be −32.4 and −28.0mV, respectively, suggesting RB-hy molecules were successfully attached on the surface of PFBT Pdots.

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared Pdots@RB-hy in the absence and presence of Cu(II) were studied to demonstrate the feasibility for Cu(II) detection with Pdots@RB-hy. As shown in Fig. 1C, the bare PFBT Pdots display a strong fluorescence emission at 544nm (curve a), which agrees with previous reports (Chan et al., 2011a; Wu and Chiu, 2013). The single-particle absorption cross section was calculated to be 6.1×10−13 cm2 according to the literature (Sun et al., 2016a) and the fluorescence quantum yield of bare PFBT Pdots was calculated to be 0.18 using a dilute solution of coumarin 6 in ethanol as reference. After assembling the PFBT Pdots with RB-hy, the obtained Pdots@RB-hy nanocomposites show same fluorescence emission at 544nm (curve c in Fig. 1C). In the presence of Cu(II), we can clearly see that Pdots@RB-hy-Cu(II) system shows two typical fluorescence emissions (curve d in Fig. 1C), one is located at 544nm and ascribed to the emission of PFBT Pdots, and the other is located at 583nm and ascribed to the emission of RB-hy-Cu(II). Comparing to the situation in the absence of Cu(II) (curve c in Fig. 1C), the emission intensity at 544nm was decreased and a new emission at 583nm appeared. Furthermore, the more Cu(II) was added, the more decreased fluorescence at 544nm and the more increased fluorescence at 583nm were observed (curve e in Fig. 1C). The observed fluorescence spectra phenomena are attributed to the FRET between PFBT Pdots and RB-hy-Cu(II) coordinated complex. As shown in Fig. 1D, RB-hy is a colorless (curve b in Fig. 1D) and non-fluorescent (curve a in Fig. 1D) reagent for highly selective coordinating with Cu(II) (Dujols et al., 1997), but its coordinated complex with Cu(II), RB-hy-Cu(II) displays typical absorption peak (curve c in Fig. 1D) and emission peak(curve e in Fig. 1D). Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of RB-hy-Cu(II) overlaps well with the emission spectrum of PFBT Pdots (curve c and d in Fig. 1D), providing the condition for the occurrence of FRET between them, in which PFBT Pdots act as energy donors while RB-hy-Cu(II) as energy acceptors. From Fig. 1D, we also can see that there is a big wavelength difference between the emission of PFBT Pdots (curve d) and the RB-hy-Cu(II) (curve e), suggesting that it′s possible to develop a ratiometric and highly selective sensor for Cu(II) with the functionalized Pdots@RB-hy.

The optimal experimental conditions including incubation time and pH have been optimized for ratiometric sensing of Cu(II) with the prepared PFBT Pdots (Fig. S1 and S2, Supporting Information). Under the optimal conditions, the ﬂuorescent spectra of Pdots@RB-hy in the presence of different concentrations of Cu(II) are collected. As displayed in Fig. 2A, we can see that the fluorescence intensities of RB-hy-Cu(II) (583nm) increase gradually while the fluorescence intensities of PFBT Pdots (544nm) decrease with increasing the concentrations of Cu(II), accompanying with color changes of the solution from green to orange under a 365nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 2A). These results demonstrate that the FRET between the formed complexes RB-hy-Cu(II) (acceptors) and PFBT Pdots (donors) occurs and therefore induce the changes of fluorescence spectra. At the concentration of 5μM Cu(II), the FRET efficiency was estimated to be 51.3% according to the literature procedure (Li et al., 2014). Based on the variations of fluorescence intensities of acceptors RB-hy-Cu(II) and donors PFBT Pdots at two wavelengths, a ratiometric sensing for Cu(II) can be achieved. The calibration curve was constructed using the emission intensity ratios of RB-hy-Cu(II) to PFBT Pdots (I 583/I 544) against the concentrations of Cu(II). As shown in Fig. 2B, a good linear correlation between the intensity ratios (I 583/I 544) and the concentrations of Cu(II) ranging from 0.05μM to 5μM with the correlation coefficients (R) of 0.9978 and a sensitivity of 0.367 per μM were obtained. The detection limit of Cu(II) was calculated to be 15nM using the 3sb/slope (S/N =3) criterion. The sensitivity was significantly superior to those of previous Cu(II) assays (Table S1, supporting information), which attributes to the signal amplification effect of the designed Pdots for quantitative assay. In addition, with increasing Cu(II) concentrations, the absorption peaks changed gradually as shown in Fig. 2C, indicating the present system can also be used to colorimetric analysis for Cu(II).

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed method for Cu(II) assay, the effects of some potential interferents including Co(II), Zn(II), Mg(II), K(I), Na(I), Ca(II), Ag(I), Cd(II), Hg(II), Fe(II), Al(III), Pb(II), Ni(II) on the relative fluorescence intensity of the analytical system were tested. As shown in Fig. 3D, upon addition of these different metal ions, the intensity ratio (I583/ I544) of sensing system shows little change compared with that in the absence of metal ions, suggesting that this proposed ratiometric sensing system is specific for the detection of Cu(II) which significantly superior to the systems based on aggregation-induced or electron transfer mechanism. The high selectivity is attributed to the special binding activity of RB-hy to Cu(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity with PFBT Pdots@RB-hy
ce:section.ce:para: As we know, pyrophosphate (PPi) is one of the natural substrates of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and can be hydrolyzed into phosphate (Pi) under the catalysis of ALP (Chen et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2016). On the other hand, Cu(II) can reversibly bind to PPi, but it can not bind to the hydrolyzed product of PPi, Pi (Liu and Schanze, 2008). On the basis of these facts, the above proposed FRET system from PFBT Pdots to RB-hy-Cu(II) will be suppressed in the presence of PPi. However, the suppression will disappear when PPi is hydrolyzed to produce Pi under the catalysis of ALP. Combining the FRET system and the ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis of PPi, the proposed PFBT Pdots@RB-hy was further extended to be served as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for ALP activity analysis.

Fig. 3A shows fluorescence spectra of the sensing system at various ALP concentrations in the presence of 5μM Cu(II) and 10μM PPi. As expected, the emission intensity of RB-hy-Cu(II) (583nm) increased gradually, while the emission intensity of PFBT Pdots (544nm) decreased gradually with increasing ALP concentrations. At the meantime, remarkable color changes of system were observed under the ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 3A, inset). As shown in Fig. 3B, the intensity ratios (I583/ I544) against ALP concentrations are linear in the range from 0.005UL−1 to 15UL−1 (R =0.996) and the detection limit was determinated to be 0.0018UL−1 with a sensitivity of 0.097 per U L−1. The analytical performances of the present sensing system are superior to those reported in literature (Table S2, supporting information). It has to be noted that, prior to detect ALP activity with the present system, the amount of the co-existed reagents including Cu(II) and PPi for assaying ALP activity was optimized to be 5 μM Cu(Ⅱ) relying on its ratiometric sensing mechanism demonstrated above, and 10μM PPi (the data are shown in Fig. S3, supporting information) using minimum intensity ratio (I583/ I544) as the optimal criterion, respectively.

In order to investigate the specificity of this fluorescent probe to ALP detection, we studied the effect of potentially interfering substances existed in the blood such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), thrombin, trypsin, immunoglobulins G (IgG), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), streptavidin (SA), and lysozyme on the assay of ALP activity. As shown in the Fig. 3C, the intensity ratios (I583/ I544) did not show obvious significant changes by these interferents. This result suggests that the proposed ratiometric sensor possesses highly specific for ALP activity analysis because of the high selectivity of RB-hy for binding Cu(II) and the specific ALP-catalyzed hydrolyzation reaction of PPi.

The excellent sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed ratiometric sensing system was further applied for assaying ALP activity in human serum samples with the standard addition methods, and the results of analysis were summarized in Table 1. The recoveries of the proposed method ranging from 95.9–102.0% were obtained, indicating that our proposed method is reliable for clinical analysis.

In summary, we have demonstrated that RB-hy modified Pdots can be used for ratiometric detection of copper ion and ALP activity via FRET mechanism. Because of the inherent characteristics of functionalized Pdots such as ampliﬁed energy transfer and high brightness of PFBT Pdots combing the ratiometric sensing mechanism, the proposed sensors have shown enhanced sensitivity and high selectivity. We believe rational surface functionalization of Pdots will pave a new way for fabricating chemo/biosensing platform.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21575004, 21605001), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-12-0599), the project sponsored by SRF for ROCS, SEM, and the Foundation for Innovation Team of Bioanalytical Chemistry of Anhui Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.12.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
